data.title: Berkshire Hathaway: Undervalued And Oversold
data.description:
My thesis

Berkshire Hathaway (NYSE:BRK.A) (NYSE:BRK.B) stock demonstrated price decline after Warren Buffett announced that he is retiring soon. Nevertheless, this safe harbor is still notably ahead of the broader equity market, with a 5.6% gain since early February. This means that my old bullish call aged well. I see no reason to switch to something other than Strong Buy because this stock is a great defensive play amid the current extremely uncertain environment. Despite some relief in President Trump’s trade wars, most of the trade deals with the largest partners are just temporary and recent rhetoric has not been that dovish. The valuation of Berkshire Hathaway is still quite attractive as well.

Berkshire Hathaway stock analysis

I start with the latest available quarterly financials, a crucial development for this widely diversified conglomerate. The company’s Q1 consolidated revenue of $89.7 billion was quite stable on a YoY basis, just slightly below the same quarter of FY2024. There was a notable decline in the company’s net operating earnings, though, a 14% decrease to $9.6 billion.

This was mostly explained by one-off events rather than some fundamental problems, as the insurance business saw a notable dip in underwriting income due to $1.1 billion in catastrophe losses from the California wildfires. This loss was partially offset by the improved investment income, from around $2.6 billion to $2.9 billion.

Another large segment that demonstrated earnings decline was “Manufacturing, service and retailing”. But the decline was immaterial, around $28 million for a $3 billion total. On the other hand, railroad earnings were up by a solid 6% YoY. Berkshire Hathaway Energy earnings also demonstrated strong growth, from $717 million to around $1.1 billion. As we can see, Berkshire’s operating segments demonstrate strength and ability to drive more value for shareholders. I am not mentioning the notable investment loss in Q1 because one quarter is just nothing for a company that has kept its investments for decades and has a very cautious approach to investing. The important part is that the company is in a massive $347 billion cash and cash equivalents position (including short-term securities), which can be used to invest in new promising names.

I am confident that this $347 billion cash pile will be used to create more value for shareholders because of the company’s stellar proven record of success. Warren Buffett is an investing legend who was able to beat the broader equity market for decades. Yes, he is retiring soon, but we already know the successor, Greg Abel. It is not a spontaneous decision because this succession plan was initially mentioned by Berkshire’s leadership four years ago. Therefore, Greg Abel had plenty of time to get prepared for the role. Moreover, he has been part of the conglomerate since 1999 when CalEnergy became part of Berkshire Hathaway and highly likely has been working very closely with Warren Buffett and Charlie Munger since 2018 when he joined the company’s board of directors. Therefore, his experience and the adherence to Berkshire’s values should not be a concern.

Besides company-specific positives, the environment also shows us various signs that betting on a high-quality safe harbor like Berkshire Hathaway is a reasonable move. There is still vast uncertainty related to the Fed’s next moves in terms of the monetary policy. The Fed remains quite hawkish and there were still no rate cuts this year despite less than a month left before the first half of the year ends. Higher interest rates for longer will certainly weigh on investors’ confidence in long-duration assets, making a defensive play like Berkshire Hathaway compelling.

The soaring gold price is another large sign of investors seeking safe assets. After some trade wars lull a few weeks ago, President Trump demonstrates some signs of returning to a more hawkish rhetoric with his recent words that China violated the temporary trade deal. In addition, there was an order signed regarding a large 50% tariffs increase on steel and aluminum. Therefore, the tariffs agenda does not look completely off the table.

Last but not least, the technical setup looks extremely good. The stock's current RSI indicator of around 35 is low, meaning that BRK.B is oversold. Berkshire's RSI indicator is rarely dips that low, which looks like an apparent bullish signal as well.

Intrinsic value calculation

Berkshire’s robust YTD performance helps the company to maintain position in the $1 trillion market cap club. I think it is fair that Berkshire’s valuation ratios are above the industry median because it is absolutely unparalleled in terms of the quality of management and scale. In addition, there is unmatched profitability that justifies higher multiples. The stellar track record of Warren Buffett against the S&P 500 since the 1960s is another crucial factor that makes Berkshire unique.

I believe that assessing Berkshire's valuation attractiveness through comparative analysis of its current multiples against historical ones is a sound approach. Based on this analysis, the stock is fairly valued because valuation ratios are very close to historical averages. This is very good because a company like Berkshire deserves a premium over the fair value.

Furthermore, Wall Street analysts covering Berkshire suggest that the stock is undervalued by around 6% because of the consensus target price of $521. The trend presented above suggests that the stock has been historically quite predictable in closing the gap between its actual share price and consensus target price. This reinforces the assertion that the stock is currently attractively valued.

What can go wrong with my thesis?

Investors are currently much more tolerant of taking risks than they were in March and April. This increases demand for riskier assets like growth stocks, meaning that safer harbors like Berkshire might lack attention from investors. I think that such a pivot in the stock market sentiment might be another reason (apart from the announcement that Warren Buffett retires next year) for the share price underperformance over the last month.

Another reason why Berkshire’s share price performance was weak in May is seasonality. This one has been historically the weakest month for this stock, with a mere 36.4% win rate. We also have to warn readers that June has not been historically stronger, with a slightly higher win rate of 40%. Therefore, the recent share price weakness might continue during the first month of Summer.

Summary

There are no reasons to change Berkshire Hathaway’s rating to something else other than Strong Buy. The fundamentals of this unparalleled safe harbor are rock-solid, and the stock is very attractively valued.

data.title: Stop Guessing Tomorrow: Insights On Long/Short Strategies
data.description:
By Marc Regenbaum

Mark Regenbaum shares lessons learned from two decades of experience managing long/short portfolios.

Transcript

Anu Rajakumar: In a world shaped by shifting policies, geopolitical tensions, and rapidly evolving market structures, uncertainty has become the new norm, bringing with it heightened volatility. For investors, the question isn't just how to weather the storm, but how to turn challenges into opportunities. Could long-short strategies hold the key as a powerful tool for diversification and risk management in these unpredictable times? What should investors look for in a manager as they look to capitalize on today's opportunities while mitigating risks?

My name is Anu Rajakumar, and joining me today is Marc Regenbaum, managing director and portfolio manager for the Neuberger Berman Long Short Fund. Marc, welcome to Disruptive Forces.

Marc Regenbaum: Thanks for having me, Anu.

Anu Rajakumar: Now, Marc, market environments always bring their own challenges, but the current mix of volatility and sector concentration seem particularly unique. How should investors think about navigating this environment, and what stands out to you about its current characteristics?

Marc Regenbaum: We like to say starting points matter. We believe we are living through a confluence of events today, with business cycle normalization, meaning businesses are competing more for market shares. We are also going through AI normalization, where some of that technology is maturing. We're doing all of this against a rapidly changing policy backdrop. These days, monetary, fiscal, and foreign policy are increasingly intertwined, leading to a very uneven choreography for policymakers themselves. All the while, as you said, we're sitting with markets at concentration levels rarely seen.

With that, we are seeing volatility and policy uncertainty real time in today's markets. April's market performance, starting with that downdraft and then the rapid recovery, was real-time evidence of what we're going to be experiencing going forward. That said, we do believe volatility can be seen as an opportunity rather than a risk, given the market structure. As active investors, we are suddenly more focused on the details and the risk management process we've employed for many years. For us as portfolio managers, the emphasis is on preparing for that high-volatility regime.

We like to say, think of how the markets have performed since the presidential election itself, because tariffs may come and go, but there will be continuous change, whether it's immigration policy, geopolitics, tax policy, you name it. Last but not least, you put it all together for investors. I think it's important for investors to take a perspective of whether they want to reset their portfolios, and really take a step back and reflect on how they felt during recent market swings. Did the portfolios really behave as they expected? Were they able to sleep at night? The magnitude of the change that we're seeing have just been magnificent, and you have an opportunity to adjust if you weren't really sleeping well over that period of time.

Anu Rajakumar: Yes, I'm sure the last few weeks and months indeed have been quite magnificent in a variety of different ways. Marc, I'm noticing that long-short strategies are increasingly gaining recognition. I recently saw articles about Norway's $1.8 trillion Sovereign Wealth Fund, announcing that they will be allocating to long-short managers. What does this signal about the role of these types of strategies in portfolios when it comes to diversifiers?

Marc Regenbaum: I think it's a great question, but I have to take a step back and say that one size does not fit all for long-short strategies. There are many different flavors of long-short strategies out there. Institutional firms have that due diligence capabilities to understand whether they're looking at a 130-30 fund or a market-neutral fund. There's very much different levels of risk taking through the likes of understanding gross exposures, net exposures, leverage, and concentration approaches of different long-short strategies.

For many of them, like ourselves, what's important, as you alluded to in the lead-in of the question, is, can you dampen volatility while still capture upside potential? That smoother ride phenomenon or make more by losing less. It's really important for investors to make sure they're aligning their goals and risk tolerance with the strategy that they're looking at. All of this can help decide whether long-short can be an anchor of your investment strategy, or just a broader piece of an overall asset allocation. Within the construct of today's environment, we truly believe that we are having a larger total addressable market for long short strategy opportunities, given increasing dispersion along the way.

Anu Rajakumar: Excellent. Now, Marc, I know you've been managing long-short strategies for a long time, almost two decades, I believe.

Marc Regenbaum: That's right.

Anu Rajakumar: It must have given you valuable insights over the last few years. You've gone through ups, downs, and everything in between. What lessons has history taught you about effectively managing these strategies?

Marc Regenbaum: It's a great question. One, if I was to take a step back and conduct my own due diligence on long-short managers, my checklist would really start with the following questions. First and foremost, does that manager have the ability to add value through their short process. Shorting is a very different strategy than doing your traditional long investing. Second, I'd make sure, is there a process and discipline in executing the strategies through changing environments? You want to understand how the portfolio would behave in different market environments.

Of course, where it should really shine is understanding the historical performance during the periods of volatility. How does one conduct that diligence? I think there are some specific questions you can actually go about to ask the managers. First, what is your long-term beta? That helps to give you so much information content of where it can fall within a broader portfolio asset allocation. Second, you could even use recent market environments, such as how did you perform during the challenging markets of 2022, and how did you perform during the recovery of the markets in 2023?

Remember, long-short strategies aren't just about protecting money, it's also about making money, and you have to actually be able to compound that capital back up to actually make our end clients some really attractive returns over time. It's really important to understand a manager's risk management philosophy. Look, I think if you take a step back in today's environment, one may even suggest that there's potentially clear lessons from the 2000 to 2010 market period. This is another one where you can see the opportunity of making attractive returns through not just the long side of the portfolio, but the short side of the portfolio as well. After all, from that 2000-2010 period, we actually lived through a decade that was basically a flat equity market environment.

Anu Rajakumar: Terrific. You mentioned, asking that question, what's your long-term beta? Just out of curiosity, what's reasonable when folks are looking at long-short managers, what are thresholds that they should really be thinking about?

Marc Regenbaum: Again, it goes back to what you were trying to achieve for your risk returns. I think the reality is, when you start to exceed betas that are in elevation of 0.6 or so, you're starting to be long only light, for lack of a better word. Of course, your classic market-neutral approaches would usually approach around 0 or + or -0.2. For us, we think a really nice tiptoeing to equity markets or dial-down from full-blown equity markets, said differently, your classic equity hedge, should really generally be in that, call it about 0.4 area.

Anu Rajakumar: Terrific. Thank you very much. Now, Marc, you've mentioned active management a couple of times so far, which, of course, active management plays a critical role in the success of long/short strategies. Could you talk about why active management is so important in this context?

Marc Regenbaum: Absolutely. One of my favorite topics, for sure.

Anu Rajakumar: You just lit up when I said that question.

Marc Regenbaum: I had to.

Anu Rajakumar: I love that.

Marc Regenbaum: It is the importance of knowing what you get from your active manager and, candidly, what you get from your passive management solutions as well, because passive is not riskless. It is absolutely something that is comprised of the underlying exposures that the market is made up of. When you think about today's world, we are just living in a world of volatility that has so much information content. If you are an active manager and you want to understand your artificial intelligence exposure, all you had to do is look at the market dynamics and how your portfolio performed on the day DeepSeek (DEEPSEEK) entered the conversation.

If you want to understand your exposure and how much you have that's related to tariffs, understand what happened after the announcement on April 2nd, and similarly, when there was a 90-day pause just a week later. You are very much seeing dynamics and details come to life across sectors and across industries that create real opportunities for the active managers. I think in today's world, the active manager debate actually is getting a little bit watered down. I appreciate the simplicity of it, but one of the most common conversations is the Magnificent 7 versus everyone else, or the remaining 493 stocks in simplicity. It's just not how we view the world.

We think the importance of this world that we're living in today is focusing on the details. It's not as simple as just top-down versus bottom-up. There are going to be individual themes that are going to have real implications for different sectors, different companies that are going to create significant performance dispersion between winners and losers, and again, even within the same sub-sectors. Of course, the cherry on a Sundae when you think about long-short within this active conversation is some of the details that come along with that strategy. The ability to earn interest income from your short proceeds, the potential tax efficiency one can have if your strategy goes down that route.

Again, bring it all together. Long-short, I'm, of course, inherently biased by speaking about this strategy, of course. We love this environment. We are passionate about it because there's so much opportunity to take advantage of active management, to take advantage of the strategy, to make more by losing less for our underlying clients, and ultimately to bring process and risk management to today's uncertain markets.

Anu Rajakumar: Yes, absolutely. No, it's so great to see you so passionate about even tough times like these. I think a lot of investors find it very difficult to stay disciplined in these kind of uncertain environments, especially with temptations of chasing trends or reacting to volatility. Humans are emotional, and that sometimes comes through in our investment decisions.

Marc Regenbaum: For sure.

Anu Rajakumar: Marc, what advice would you give to investors on maintaining discipline during times like these?

Marc Regenbaum: You were spot on. For better or worse, I love this market backdrop. This is the type of market backdrop, I think, where process and risk management and experience comes to life. It's very different than what I like to call the FOMO markets, or the fear of missing out, which one might make the case was happening about 12 to 18 months ago. I think where I'd start the conversation, though, is when you see volatility and you see uncertainty, the first rule is stop trying to guess tomorrow.

Guessing can very much lead to investors to compounding mistakes because you can be whipsawed very quickly. Again, April was a great example of that because it would've proved very expensive if you sold at the low and then never got back in into the underlying markets themselves. Again, the importance of having a process and sticking to it during unpredictable markets or unpredictable times is paramount in today's world. After all, it's not easy.

We are absolutely inundated with information each day, and sometimes the very hardest thing to do is actually to stay the course. For us, it's about understanding process. That process itself is informed by our experience. Then, very importantly for our investors, we're hoping to use the volatility to our advantage so we can focus on their long-term goals and make attractive risk-adjusted returns throughout this uncertainty.

Anu Rajakumar: Absolutely. Uncertainty in the market doesn't just impact investors. It has broader implications for companies as well. What are some of the key challenges or adjustments that you're seeing companies face during these uncertain times?

Marc Regenbaum: I said the other was my favorite question. This might actually be something where I'll really get too excited to speak about. I love the bottom-up analysis and meeting with the companies to understand the real-world implications that we're trying to decipher as investors. The reality of the situation is that the risk management principles for companies very much parallel those that we as investors are trying to navigate, except it's probably harder. The CEOs themselves are actually the chief capital allocators. They have to focus on those balance sheets and those cash flows.

Of course, relative to investors like myself and my team, where you have liquid investment strategies, their ability to shift course is much more difficult, especially since many of their projects are very much longer lived. The challenges of today's backdrop faced by the corporations are managing supply chains, managing customer expectations, managing capital allocation in these uncertain environments. As we discussed earlier, the myriad of policy changes on the table are extremely vast, and they're significant, and that extends beyond the tariffs. The magnitude and implementation approach matters greatly, and these management teams are having to dig into the details of their own portfolios to understand how they want to adjust.

We like to say what's been going on is, as the companies are waiting to hear the rules of the actual game that we're playing today, investors are hoping for the pause that refreshes. We are seeing this real time, again, I spoke about sub-sectors, but you get very different dynamics if you are a company such as Walmart (WMT) and how they're adapting to policy changes with tariffs and their underlying mix of business, which is a little bit more consumables as opposed to discretionary products versus even their closest competitor such as Target (TGT), which has a little bit more of a product category mix that has a little bit more discretionary in terms of the nature of what their customers are buying.

Know the company's products, know their geographies, know the channels, the customer base, the pricing power of the underlying businesses. This is the stuff that active managers were trained for, that they yearn for, and now they have to actually put it to work. Combining all of that with the details of balance sheet strengths, which oftentimes equity investors overlook, can really determine how aggressive individual companies can be. Again, that can determine how much alpha generation the underlying securities can deliver as well.

Anu Rajakumar: All right. That's terrific. I think we'll end our comments there on that high note, I think, Marc. Before you go, I have to ask you one quick bonus question. I'd love to know, Marc, what is your guilty pleasure or hobby that most people wouldn't expect from you?

Marc Regenbaum: Oh, boy. That is a surprise question. I will tell you. I think what most people don't appreciate from me is, while I tend to invest in a lot of healthy foods and companies, I believe in the secular trend. I might be the unhealthiest eater that is out there. I absolutely love--

Anu Rajakumar: What's your fast food place of choice, for example?

Marc Regenbaum: Gosh. I'm a heavy chicken parm pizza and bagels kind of person. From that kind of dynamic, on the other side, I will eat in some of the-- I'll bounce it out with a little bit of Sweetgreen and McDonald's and just salad as well. I'm open to all.

Anu Rajakumar: The heart wants what the heart wants.

Marc Regenbaum: Exactly. Risk management and balance.

Anu Rajakumar: Exactly. Absolutely. Thank you for sharing that and for your insights today, Marc. In today's conversation, you really emphasized the critical role that long-short strategies can play in managing volatility and potentially capturing some upside potential as well. A few quotes that I wrote down that I really liked that you said, number one, one size does not fit all when it comes to long short strategies. You said it's important to align strategies with investor goals and risk tolerances.

Another line that you said passive is not riskless, which I think is an important reminder that we have to have. Then lastly, stop guessing tomorrow, I love that you talked about the importance of having a robust process and sticking to it, even, or I should say, especially in uncertain times as we're facing at the moment. Thank you again for being here and for sharing those thoughts today.

Marc Regenbaum: Great to be here. Thank you so much.

data.title: Wayfair: Debt Refinancing Leads To Rising Interest Costs
data.description:
Wayfair's (NYSE:W) Q1 2025 net revenues ended up at the high end of expectations. Combined with its contributed progress with cost reductions, that allowed Wayfair to report a $31 million improvement in adjusted EBITDA compared to Q1 2024.

Wayfair also issued $700 million in secured notes and redeemed most of its 2026 convertible notes. It appears well positioned to deal with its $393 million in remaining 2025 and 2026 convertible notes with its $1.338 billion in cash and cash equivalents (as of the end of Q1 2025).

Wayfair's interest costs are increasing by close to $50 million per year from its debt moves, but it should still be able to generate around $150 million in free cash flow during 2025. This is with the benefit in paying out over $300 million in equity-based compensation instead of cash, though.

I am keeping my estimate of Wayfair's intrinsic value at $27 per share. This reflects its good progress with cost reductions, balanced out with some concerns around the impact of tariffs. Wayfair has a diverse range of suppliers on its platform, so that helps mitigate the impact of tariffs. However, any slowdown in the US economy would affect demand.

Wayfair currently appears overvalued, although its balance sheet should be fine over the next couple of years if consumer demand doesn't plummet.

New Secured Notes

In March, Wayfair issued $700 million in 7.75% secured notes due September 2030. The $691 million in net proceeds from this offering mainly went to repurchasing most of its outstanding 1.0% convertible notes due 2026.

Wayfair repurchased $578 million of those notes for $551 million, a roughly 5% discount to par.

This left it with $156 million in remaining 1.0% convertible notes due 2026 as well as $237 million in 0.625% convertible notes due 2025.

Wayfair reported $1.338 billion in cash and cash equivalents at the end of Q1 2025, so it should be easily able to repay its remaining 2025 and 2026 convertible notes with cash on hand.

Wayfair also has $1.38 billion in outstanding 2027 and 2028 convertible notes, but there is still potential for those notes to be converted into shares. Wayfair's current share price is relatively close to the conversion price ($63.45 for the 2027 convertible notes and $45.80 for the 2028 convertible notes) for those notes, while its 2025 and 2026 convertible notes had much higher conversion prices and thus needed to be repaid. Wayfair's interest costs are increasing, with its latest moves adding close to $50 million per year in interest costs. It now has around $120 million in net interest costs per year.

If it needs to refinance its 2027 and 2028 notes in the future (if its share price isn't high enough for conversion), then its net interest costs could approach $200 million per year.

Q1 2025 Results

Wayfair's Q1 2025 results ended up slightly better than expectations overall.

It expected flat to slightly negative net revenue growth for Q1 2025 and delivered essentially flat (up $1 million) net revenue growth compared to Q1 2024. Wayfair's net revenue growth would have been +1% without its exit from Germany, including +1.6% net revenue growth from its US operations.

Wayfair's Q1 2025 gross margins were also solid at 30.7%, slightly above the midpoint of its guidance for 30% to 31% gross margins during the quarter and up from 30.0% in Q1 2024.

Wayfair has also made continued progress in reducing its operating costs, leading to it reporting $106 million in adjusted EBITDA for a 3.9% adjusted EBITDA margin. This compares to $75 million in adjusted EBITDA in Q1 2024, with a 2.7% adjusted EBITDA margin.

Near-Term Outlook

If Wayfair can maintain flat net revenue growth during the rest of 2025, it should end up meeting my prior expectation for $500 million to $550 million in 2025 adjusted EBITDA. I currently expect approximately $530 million in adjusted EBITDA in a flat net revenue scenario for 2025.

Wayfair has over 20,000 suppliers on its platform now, roughly double what it had in 2019. The diversity of that supplier base should help reduce the impact of tariffs, although with 10% baseline tariffs, there may be a minor impact nonetheless.

I am modeling Wayfair's net revenue growth at flat for the rest of 2025, but there is probably more downside risk (especially if tariffs escalate) there than upside potential.

Wayfair may be able to generate around $150 million in free cash flow in 2025, although that is with over $300 million in expected equity-based compensation. If it paid that compensation in cash instead, Wayfair would be burning over $150 million in cash instead.

Notes On Valuation

I am keeping my estimate of Wayfair's intrinsic value at $27 per share. Wayfair's Q1 2025 results were generally positive, with net revenues at the high end of expectations and continued progress in cost-cutting. Wayfair has also addressed its near-term (2025 and 2026) debt maturities with the issuance of new secured notes due 2030.

The new secured notes do add to Wayfair's interest costs, though, and it is only able to generate free cash flow through the substantial use of equity-based compensation. Also, while I am modeling Wayfair's net revenue growth as flat through the rest of 2025, an increase in tariffs could easily push that negative.

A $27 per share value for Wayfair gives it an enterprise value of approximately 10x its 2025 adjusted EBITDA. That is with equity-based compensation removed though, so with equity-based compensation included, Wayfair's EV to EBITDA multiple would be around 25x at a $27 per share value.

Conclusion

Wayfair issued $700 million in secured notes to help redeem its near-term convertible notes. Wayfair's interest costs are increasing, but it should still be able to generate around $150 million in free cash flow during 2025, albeit with the use of over $300 million in equity-based compensation.

I estimate its intrinsic value at $27 per share (with some favorable assumptions), so I'd consider Wayfair fairly overvalued currently, even assuming that it can keep net revenue flat. There is also a risk that the macro environment worsens and Wayfair's net revenues end up declining.

data.title: Forget 'TACO' And Buy SCHD
data.description:
There are plenty of trading strategies, including the ‘TACO trade’ that was recently coined by Robert Armstrong of the Financial Times. In short, this trading strategy involves buying the dip in stocks when the market is fearful around tariff announcements, and then selling those stocks on a rebound when said policy proposals are reversed or put on hold.

This strategy, however, is easier said than done, as those investors who can properly time the market are few and far between. That’s why I’d rather buy attractively valued stocks that pay a decent yield while giving me meaningful upside potential. A hidden benefit of this strategy comes from the avoidance of having to pay short-term capital gains taxes, which are incurred when one frequently trades in and out of stocks for a quick gain.

Buying right and holding tight on a wide basket of dividend stocks enables you to pursue other worldly interests while putting your capital to work for you in a low-stress way, and this brings me to the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (NYSEARCA:SCHD).

I last covered SCHD in April, highlighting its attractive fee structure and holdings, all while delivering a great starting yield. SCHD remains attractively valued, as it’s ‘only’ risen by 6.6% since my last visit, sitting below the 20% rise in the S&P 500 (SPY) over the same timeframe. In this article, I discuss why SCHD remains a great pick for income and value, so let’s get started!

Why SCHD?

SCHD is a managed ETF by Charles Schwab (SCHW) that aims to replicate the performance of the Dow Jones U.S. Dividend 100 index. SCHD rebalances quarterly in order to maintain exposure to desired sectors. This generally results in a tilt toward value, as some stock holdings’ valuations may grow and become expensive.

As such, SCHD’s top holdings are comprised of stocks that mostly trade well under a forward PE of 20x while paying a decent and well-covered yield. Top industries for SCHD include Consumer Defensive, Energy, Healthcare, and Industrials, which comprise 64% of the portfolio total. High-dividend-yielding Verizon (VZ) leads the pack as SCHD’s top holding. Others include strong dividend payers like Coca-Cola (KO), Altria (MO), Lockheed Martin, (LMT) and ConocoPhillips (COP), as shown below.

While these are not hot stocks like Nvidia (NVDA), they offer investors far more diversification through sitting in SCHD’s portfolio compared to a single stock purchase, all while trading at far more appealing valuations. This leads to a far more appealing dividend yield than that of the S&P 500.

As shown below, SCHD’s 4% yield compared to SPY’s 1.2% yield currently sits at one of the widest gaps over the past 10 years. Notably, SCHD’s 10-year dividend CAGR of 11.3% is far higher than the 6.2% 10-year CAGR of SPY.

At the same time, SCHD carries a Beta score of less than 1x, which means that it is less volatile than the market average. This can give investors much-needed downside protection during uncertain times, as the market currently finds itself.

Moreover, it’s not as if SCHD’s holdings are slouches either. This is reflected by top holding, Verizon, beating earnings estimates during Q1 2025 while also demonstrating revenue growth through gaining broadband customers. At the same time, Coca-Cola saw unit case volume growth of 2% YoY and reported higher organic sales compared to peers PepsiCo (PEP) and Keurig Dr Pepper (KDP) during Q1. Moreover, Dividend Aristocrat Altria (MO) beat on earnings during Q1 and is seeing robust growth in next-generation products such as on!

Unlike actively managed ETFs, SCHD’s passively managed structure results in far lower fees. This is reflected by SCHD’s low expense ratio of just 0.06%. This sits far below the 0.5% median across the ETF universe and is even lower than the 0.09% expense ratio of SPY. Plus, due to SCHD being widely held, it scores an A+ for Liquidity, meaning that investors don’t have to worry about wide price spreads when buying and selling the ETF.

Risks to SCHD include the potential for tariffs to hit the profitability of some of the multinational companies in SCHD’s portfolio. Moreover, drug price negotiations with Medicare could introduce uncertainty in SCHD’s pharmaceutical holdings such as Amgen (AMGN), which is a top 10 holding. A competitive telco pricing environment could also negatively impact top-holding Verizon.

Considering all the above, I believe SCHD remains a strong value proposition at the current price of $26.23, which, as shown below, trades in the bottom half of its 52-week range. Given the overall defensive nature of the portfolio combined with solid growth profiles in its top holdings, SCHD presents a solid value proposition at the current price. This, combined with the 4% yield (based on TTM dividends of $1.04 per share), makes SCHD a solid pick for income and value investors in this volatile market.

Investor Takeaway

SCHD remains a compelling choice for income-focused investors seeking stability, value, and long-term upside potential. It has a high-quality portfolio of dividend-paying stocks across defensive sectors like Consumer Staples, Energy, and Healthcare. SCHD also offers a 4% yield, which is significantly above that of the S&P 500, paired with strong dividend growth and lower volatility.

Lastly, SCHD’s very low expense ratio and passive structure make it a cost-effective way to gain diversified exposure without the stress of timing the market. At its current price in the bottom half of its 52-week range, SCHD presents a wonderful opportunity for investors looking to build wealth through steady income and long-term capital appreciation potential.

data.title: Power Up Your Portfolio With Reliable Dividends; Yields +10%
data.description:
Co-authored with Hidden Opportunities

No one truly knows what the future holds. Whether it’s the outcome of a football game or the weather forecast, we can make reasonable projections based on available data, but there are no guarantees. Unexpected events can quickly derail even the most informed predictions. You might confidently guess the winner of tonight’s game or tomorrow’s weather, but your opinions will be dismissed if you claim to know who will win the 2026 Super Bowl or forecast next year’s weather down to the day.

The same principle applies to interest rates. Today, analysts and economists broadly expect at least two rate cuts this year. That may prove accurate – or not. But even the most seasoned economists wouldn’t risk predicting where rates will be two or three years from now. Even if they do, you shouldn’t listen to them.

We don’t know what the future holds for us, but we know that we will need income to support ourselves tomorrow, next week, next month, and next year. We discuss two picks that are capable of supporting a rich income stream, whether rates are maintained, cut, or raised.

Let’s dive in.

Pick #1: JPC – Yield 10.1%

The King of Bonds, Jeffrey Gundlach, expects at least two rate cuts in 2025, driven by a combination of inflation data, employment metrics, and market liquidity. The acclaimed fixed-income investor projects liquidity issues in the economy, referencing Harvard University’s need to tap the bond markets for operating cash due to its high allocation in private equity and credit.

With a weakening dollar, overvalued equities, and a slowing economy, the current macro environment favors a more defensive stance. Fixed-income offers compelling risk-adjusted returns, particularly as many bonds trade at discounts due to elevated interest rates. Real yields remain positive, allowing investors to preserve and grow purchasing power while reducing exposure to equity volatility. Short- to intermediate-duration bonds help mitigate interest rate risk, while higher-quality issuers provide stability amid economic uncertainty. For investors seeking predictable income and potential capital appreciation, fixed-income now serves as both a shield and a source of steady return.

With 79.4% of its invested assets carrying investment-grade ratings, Nuveen Preferred & Income Opportunities Fund (JPC) provides deeply diversified exposure to high-quality preferred securities. The CEF (Closed-End Fund) comprises 279 holdings, with $4.1 billion in managed assets, and ~76% of the portfolio is built with securities from the financial services space, namely diversified banks, capital markets, insurance, and regional banks. Overall, JPC’s portfolio is largely built with securities issued by companies with stringent regulatory requirements. Source

The CEF’s high degree of diversification is seen from the fact that its top five positions only comprise 7.7% of its invested assets. JPC’s portfolio has a leverage-adjusted effective duration of 5.82 years, with a staggered call exposure over this period. We note that just because a preferred is callable doesn’t mean it will be called immediately.

JPC pays monthly distributions of $0.0665/share, representing a 10.1% annualized yield. Its portfolio is invested in sectors that typically produce qualified dividend income, and JPC’s distributions have been more than 90% qualified dividend income.

The CEF operates with ~38% leverage, which carries an Overnight Bank Funding Rate (OBFR) plus 0.85%, making it inherently floating and well-positioned to benefit from declining rates. In fact, rate cuts will be a strong tailwind for the CEF in the form of a natural valuation boost for its holdings and reduced interest expenses. Today, JPC trades at par with NAV, making it an attractive entry point into this defensive yet discounted asset class in a frothy common equity market and a weakening economy.

Pick #2: BIZD – Yield 11.2%

With a dual mandate of full-employment and stable prices, the Fed’s job has become more difficult amidst the U.S. trade policy shifts, as major retailers have begun issuing warnings about higher prices. The central bank’s recent comments raise the possibility of a stagflationary scenario that has largely been absent from the U.S. economy since the early 80s.

Industries directly impacted by tariffs between major trade partners include the global supply chain, manufacturing, and automotive. There are a few industries that will be indirectly impacted, and this includes consumer and retail. Analysts expect US-focused software, business services, and health-care-oriented companies to be less affected, and interestingly, this forms the top industry exposures for BDCs (Business Development Companies).

According to Fitch Ratings, the U.S. BDC sector has limited exposure to industries that are directly affected by tariffs. Notably, the sector’s direct exposure to first-order industries that will be most affected by tariffs, including manufacturing, industrial, distribution, and auto, is limited to less than 10% of the portfolios, on average, at fair value.

From the above chart, we see our holdings like Ares Capital Corp (ARCC), SLR Capital Corp (SLRC), and Blue Owl Capital Corp (OBDC) featuring among the least impacted by trade policy uncertainties. Also, the more diversified BDCs with larger exposure to first lien, senior secured debt will navigate any secondary effects of tariffs, like rising non-accruals.

Here comes a twist in the tale.

“The economy itself is still in solid shape”

– Jerome Powell

This indicates that it is less likely for rates to drop sharply in the near term. BDCs mostly issue floating-rate debt and recognize the sensitivity of their earnings streams to rates. Most of them have notably not increased their dividend payouts as much as their earnings have grown in recent years. Even if base rates decline 200bps from the 2024 peak, BDCs will still cover their regular dividend with their investment earnings. Moreover, top-tier BDCs have maintained dividend payout ratios of less than 100% of earnings for several quarters, accumulating substantial spillover income (equivalent to almost 3-4 quarterly dividends) that will continue to support payouts in upcoming quarters with a comfortable cushion. We also expect upcoming rate cuts to spur higher loan origination, generating additional fee income for BDCs. With average leverage in the sector remaining relatively low, these companies are well-positioned to capitalize on the resulting opportunities.

VanEck BDC Income ETF (BIZD) lets you invest in the entire BDC sector through a single ETF (open-ended, Exchange-Traded Fund). This fund is market-cap weighted across 30 holdings, with its top ten positions being the ten largest publicly traded BDCs, representing 76% of the invested assets. Source

The ETF does not employ leverage in its investment strategy, instead collecting dividends from its underlying BDC holdings and distributing them to shareholders. A retail investor with the time and energy can certainly replicate what BIZD does by simply buying all the public BDCs in a market-cap weighted manner and adjusting their allocations prudently to collect an optimal yield. This is easier said than done, and BIZD does this for us at a modest 0.42% expense ratio.

I want to reiterate what we have discussed several times before. BIZD’s expense is not 13.33% like the prospectus says. When it comes to FOFs (Fund of Funds), the expense ratio can be a bit misleading due to SEC requirements. Source

The net expense is broken down into direct and indirect expenses. Direct expenses are those expenses and fees that accrue daily and are paid monthly. These expenses are borne directly by the fund and reduce its net assets, thus detracting from total return. Management fees, salaries, and bonuses are part of direct expenses.

SEC requires FOFs to report a total expense ratio in their prospectus fee table that accounts for the expense ratios of the underlying funds (in this case, the underlying BDCs) in which it invests as an expense item called Acquired Fund Fees and Expenses. AFFEs are not accrued daily, nor are they paid directly from the Fund’s net assets. Despite BIZD’s net expense ratio showing 13.33%, investors should note that only 0.42% comprises direct expenses. Dividends are paid after factoring in these expenses. Please read more about this here: BIZD Expenses Explained.

Since its inception in 2014, BIZD has paid steady yet visibly variable dividends, while maintaining a stable market price.

We see the current dip as an opportunity to buy into, as BDCs have and will continue to support the U.S. middle markets through thick and thin, all while paying large dividends to investors.

Conclusion

No one can predict with certainty where interest rates will go – not in three months, not by year-end, and certainly not two or three years from now. That’s why we focus on building a rate-agnostic portfolio – one designed to deliver consistent cash flows, regardless of market conditions.

“Nobody can predict interest rates, the future direction of the economy or the stock market. Dismiss all such forecasts and concentrate on what’s actually happening to the companies in which you’ve invested.” - Peter Lynch

This article discusses BIZD and JPC. The former is largely allocated to blue-chip BDCs, with deeply diversified portfolios and significant capability of scaling up leverage to support an ailing economy, while the latter is largely composed of investment-grade preferreds from the financial services industry. Together, these enrich your income stream, no matter where interest rates are headed.

This is the essence of our Income Method, and the beauty of income investing.

data.title: MongoDB: Sandbagging Earnings (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
The last time we discussed MongoDB Inc. (MDB) "MDB," it was March and the company just reported earnings. While the earnings were solid, the guidance was disappointing, and the stock fell hard. I rated the stock a hold then, as it seemed the upside potential was limited after reporting poor guidance. Also, the post-liberation day smackdown caused MDB's stock to crater by another 30%, before MDB finally bottomed around the $140 level.

MDB 1-Year Chart

MDB recently reported earnings and the results were actually really good, especially when compared with sandbagged guidance last quarter. In fact, it seems that MDB likely lowballed guidance considerably due to increased uncertainty last quarter.

For Q1, MDB beat both top and bottom-line estimates, but perhaps most importantly, MDB guided higher. Due to the better than anticipated results, and the stock still being well off its highs, I am upgrading MDB's stock to a buy from my hold rating. I am also increasing my 2025 year-end price target from $250 to $285.

MDB's Excellent Earnings Announcement

MongoDB reported Q1 Non-GAAP EPS of $1.00, beating the consensus estimate by $0.34, smashing the consensus estimate by over 50%. Revenue came in at $549M, a $21.5M beat, and a considerable 22% YoY increase.

These results are fascinating, because MDB guided to only $524-529M in sales for Q1 around three months ago. So, MDB beat its own guidance by over 4%, which is very significant, illustrating that MDB likely sandbagged guidance due to limited visibility and tariff related uncertainty.

While I believed MDB sandbagged guidance in the previous quarter, I still had to go with a hold rating because I had no way to prove the company deliberately downplayed its growth and earnings capacity because of the transitory challenges in the economic landscape.

Nonetheless, here we are, and MDB is doing considerably better than perceived. We talked about sales, but look at MDB's earnings. The company guided to an EPS mid-point of roughly $0.65 per share. Yet, it delivered EPS of $1, smashing the consensus EPS estimate and its own lowballed guidance by about 50%.

Additionally, MDB increased its buyback program to $1 billion, and offered solid Q2 guidance. The visibility is clearly much better here for MDB than it was several months ago.

MDB's Very Solid Q2 and Full-Year Guidance

The company offered a high-end estimate of $553M for Q2 and $2.29B for the year, suggesting it could surpass the consensus estimates. However, the real progress appears to be on the earnings side, as MDB guided to a Q2 mid-point of $0.64 vs. the consensus figure of just $0.58. Additionally, for the full year, MDB plans to deliver about $3.05 on EPS this year, vs. the lowballed consensus figure of just $2.65.

MDB's Valuation Still Appears Attractive Here

MDB has a remarkable history of beating consensus estimates. In fact, I was quite surprised when doing the research here, as most high-quality mega-cap tech stocks don't even have the track record MDB has achieved.

MDB has not missed on earnings or EPS estimates in at least the last five years. MDB's TTM consensus EPS estimate was only about $2.50. However MDB delivered $4.14 instead, illustrating a very considerable 65% EPS outperformance rate.

Now, with this kind of consistent track record, there is no reason to believe that MDB's outperformance will suddenly stop. No, instead, the company can continue to outperform, in my view.

Next year's consensus EPS estimate is only about $3.69. However, this number is likely lowballed, and the higher end EPS estimates range to around $5.50, and I think we can see the estimates lifted as we advance.

The Bottom-Line

As MDB delivered $4.14, it may not have a problem delivering $5.50 in EPS next year. Given that MDB still trades around $225, its forward P/E ratio may be about 40 here, which is relatively inexpensive for an AI software company growing at a very fast pace.

On a forward P/S basis, MDB has a ratio of about 7, also relatively inexpensive for a solid AI company in this market. So, the valuation seems attractive here, and there is a high probability for positive EPS and sales growth revisions after these excellent results.

Due to the solid performance, MDB is a buy again, in my view, and I lifted the year-end price target to $285, representing about a 27% upside potential. We could also see this stock appreciate more considerably in a bullish case outcome.

Risks To MDB

MDB faces completion from Microsoft, Amazon, and other tech giants as well as smaller players in the space. Therefore, competition is a serious threat. Additionally, MDB faces risks due to the potential for a slower than anticipated economic atmosphere. Tariffs and other destabilizing factors could also impact MDB's stock negatively. MDB also faces company-specific challenges like execution risks and other elements. Investors should consider these and other risks before investing in MDB's stock.

data.title: TotalEnergies: Undervalued, Underappreciated, But Positioned To Outperform
data.description:
TotalEnergies SE (NYSE:TTE) is a global energy company which seems to be neglected by the market, despite its rare combination of undervaluation, operational resilience and strategic clarity. With double-digit shareholder returns, disciplined capital allocation and a credible path to low-carbon profitability, the company is positioning itself as a European super major that not only manages the energy transition, but monetizes it. This article examines whether the difference between market pricing and business fundamentals creates a compelling entry point for long-term investors.

Company Fundamentals and Positioning

TotalEnergies operates with a model that spans the entire energy value chain from upstream oil and gas exploration to downstream refining and petrochemicals, as well as marketing, trading and increasingly, power generation and energy storage. These five activities give an operational flexibility and cash flow stability. When refining margins shrink, upstream profits can compensate; when oil prices fall, trading or power generation activities can compensate. This diversification enables the company to maintain a high level of profitability throughout the commodity cycles.

Although roughly 80% of its revenues still come from oil and gas, but the company is actively pivoting. The Integrated Power division, which includes renewable electricity generation, storage, and trading, already accounts for approximately 8% of group turnover and is expected to reach 15-20% by 2030. This unit is expected to generate a return on average capital employed (ROACE) above 12% by the end of the decade, a target aligned with the Group's fossil assets. That ambition is not theoretical, and it is already backed by a pipeline of over 35 GW in committed capacity, across solar, wind, and energy storage platforms and Its target 100 TWh of green electricity by 2030, up from 33 TWh today.

TotalEnergies is present all over the world by starting with Africa and Middle East where the company leverages cost-effective production in long-standing partnerships in Nigeria, Angola, Mozambique, Iraq, and the UAE. In Europe, it maintains strategic dominance across refining, distribution, and storage infrastructure, while in North America the company is scaling LNG capabilities via partnerships in NextDecade and Cameron LNG, and expanding renewables through acquisitions like Clearway and offshore wind projects along the U.S. coasts. Due to this geographic repartition, the Group is able to mitigate geopolitical risks by reallocating output flexibly, and diversifying exposure across currencies and demand profiles.

In liquefied natural gas (LNG), TotalEnergies holds approximately 10% of the global market share. With a fully integrated model from upstream production (Mozambique LNG, Papua LNG), to liquefaction infrastructure (Cameron, Qatar North Field), to proprietary shipping and customer delivery, it makes a reliable long-term supplier to both Asia and Europe. Today, LNG has become a strategic growth vector, benefiting from structural demand post-2022 and serving as a transitional fuel aligned with decarbonization policies.

On the oil side, The Group holds exploration permits for strategic reserves, which include Lake Albert in Uganda and the Suriname offshore basin. The proven reserves from these locations are sufficient for eleven years of production at current levels with upstream production costs remaining under $5 per barrel, far below those of many independent producers, reinforcing its cost leadership.

TotalEnergies has achieved a moderate yet steady growth with revenue increasing from $176 billion to $237 billion between 2019 and 2023 which represents a 7.7% compound annual growth rate (CAGR) over the period and despite significant challenges with the pandemic, supply shocks, and commodity volatility. For 2024, revenue remained above $200 billion, down 10.6% year-over-year due to weaker hydrocarbon prices and refining margins. And for the first quarter of 2025 revenue was $47.9 billion, indicating an 8% annual decline year -over- year under comparable circumstances, but for me, these figures clearly reflect the Group's resilience exposure to macroeconomic cycles.

Contrary to U.S. peers like ExxonMobil or Chevron, which are still heavily fossil-fuel dependent, TotalEnergies stands out with one of the most credible and capital-backed energy transition strategies among supermajors. The Group is already executing this shift through large-scale projects in offshore wind (UK, South Korea), solar (India, Texas, Spain), and emerging hydrogen and storage initiatives. This dual focus enables TotalEnergies to leverage hydrocarbon profitability while building long-term clean energy optionality.

Financial Quality and Valuation

In a less favorable environment than 2022-2023, the Group continues to demonstrate financial discipline and generate strong free cash flow. For the first quarter of 2025, adjusted net income reached $4.2 billion, down 18% from the previous year due to lower oil prices, margin compression and seasonal slowdown in the gas segment. However, operating cash flow saw an 18% increase, reaching $2.56 billion due to an enhanced working capital dynamics and resilience across the business. And the Free cash flow after organic investments was $2.5 billion, which was sufficient to fully fund the quarterly dividend and the first $2 billion tranche of the share repurchase program without increasing debt or compromising allocations for future growth.

Net debt rose to $20.1 billion, lifting the net debt/EBITDA ratio to 1.46x, a level still well within comfort zones and largely the result of proactive shareholder returns rather than operational strain. Profitability remains strong, with ROE at 11.7%, ROIC at 8.4%, and a 13.1% free cash flow yield, placing the Group among the most cash-productive names in its peer set.

This financial strength translates into a sustainable and attractive shareholder return profile. Indeed, the current dividend yield of ~5.5% is backed by stable LNG earnings and conservative capex planning. The 5% dividend hike in early 2025 reflects a deliberate long-term policy, not a short-term windfall. It complements an $8 billion buyback program executed in 2024, signaling management's intent to return surplus capital while preserving strategic flexibility. In a sector often defined by volatility, TotalEnergies offers a rare combination: resilient income, disciplined reinvestment, and credible growth optionality.

TotalEnergies continues to trade at significantly lower multiples than its main peers, despite comparable margins and cash flow. The stock pays around 9.3 times 2024 earnings, compared with 13.7 times for ExxonMobil, 15.9 times for Chevron and over 14 times for Shell. On an EV/EBITDA basis, the multiple of 4.4 times is also well below that of the US majors (over 6.5), and in line with Shell, which shares a more integrated approach and comparable energy diversification. The market therefore seems to be applying a structural discount to TotalEnergies, a discount that can be explained neither by operational underperformance nor by excessive debt nor even by lower profitability. Rather, it seems to reflect a geographical and regulatory bias against the European majors, as well as persistent skepticism about the transition to low-carbon energies.

To complement the relative valuation, I built a five-year DCF model based on a Free Cash Flow to the Firm (FCFF) approach, anchored in a Brent price scenario of $65. The assumptions reflect a prudent yet realistic framework: a stable EBIT margin around 10%, annual capex maintained at $17 billion in line with the company's long-term investment guidance, and a discount rate (WACC) of 7.5% to capture both geopolitical and sectoral risks. Depreciation and amortization were set conservatively at $13 billion per year, and a 25% effective tax rate was applied to reflect TotalEnergies' global exposure. Using a perpetual growth rate of 1.5% beyond 2030, the model yields an enterprise value of $186 billion and an equity value of approximately $184.6 billion. With 2.3 billion shares outstanding, the resulting intrinsic value stands at $80.26 per share, implying a 34% upside from current market levels. This valuation reinforces the view that the stock is significantly undervalued even under cautious assumptions, and that the market is not fully pricing in the long-term earnings stability and cash generation capacity of the group in its division.

Catalysts and Risks

It's important to take in consideration some catalyst in the investment case of TotalEnergies over the short-medium term. The first and perhaps most impactful catalyst is the continued global build-out of the Group's liquefied natural gas (LNG) platform, which has become increasingly strategic since 2022 in light of geopolitical realignment and Europe's accelerated shift away from Russian pipeline gas.

The company is involved in several cornerstone LNG projects that already contributed and will continue to contribute meaningfully to earnings and free cash flow from 2026 onwards firstly with Mozambique LNG project, which represents a capacity of 13 million tonnes per annum (MTPA), which is gradually being reactivated after a prolonged halt due to regional security concerns. In the United States, the Cameron LNG Phase 2 expansion co-financed with Sempra Energy, will add export optionality at the heart of the global Atlantic basin gas trade, and it's important to remember that, in 2023, TotalEnergies signed a 20-year offtake agreement for 1,5 MTPA from the Rio Grande LNG project in Texas, further anchoring its position as a long-term supplier to Europe and Asia. The Group has also secured 6.3 MTPA through its participation in Qatar's North Field East megaproject, with deliveries set to begin in 2026. Moreover, all these projects are structured around long-term contracts indexed to Henry Hub prices plus spreads, which ensure a visible and recurring cash flow profile that enhances balance sheet resilience. In addition to that, natural gas is experiencing strong structural demand growth in Asia, particularly in fast-developing economies such as India, Vietnam, and the Philippines, where it serves as a transitional fuel within decarbonization roadmaps. For me, these elements support the strategic rationale behind TotalEnergies' LNG build out and provides a long demand tail for its future cargoes.

The second major catalyst is the scaling of the Integrated Power division, which now sits at the heart of the Group's diversification strategy. Indeed, TotalEnergies aims to become a global utility-scale player with a target of producing 100 terawatt-hours of electricity annually by 2030, a level comparable to leading European utilities such as Iberdrola or Enel. The Group has publicly stated its ambition to deliver a return on average capital employed (ROACE) above 12% for this business line by the end of the decade, a level significantly above traditional utility returns. If achieved, such performance would likely trigger a re-evaluation of the stock's valuation framework, gradually closing the gap between oil-centric and hybrid energy models and allowing TotalEnergies to earn valuation multiples more consistent with integrated infrastructure companies.

The third thematic catalyst is the normalization of macroeconomic and oil market conditions. Today, TotalEnergies still trades at a visible discount to U.S. peers, a gap that is less about fundamentals and more about market perception. Factors such as sluggish Chinese growth, oil price volatility, and a general skepticism toward European energy majors have contributed to this valuation gap. Yet, several developments could begin to close it. A stabilization in real interest rates could revalue long-duration infrastructure assets, particularly in the renewables space. A Brent Crude price anchored in the $70 to $80 range would mechanically support free cash flow, especially given the Group's low upstream breakeven. And a reduction in tensions between OPEC and the U.S. would ease political risk premiums currently embedded in sector valuations. Under such a central macro scenario, the persistent valuation discount applied to TotalEnergies relative to ExxonMobil or Chevron would appear increasingly unwarranted.

However, alongside these catalysts, TotalEnergies remains exposed to several key risks and the first is commodity price risk. Even despite its diversification, TotalEnergies remains exposed to oil and gas price cycles. And a sustained Brent price below $65 per barrel would materially compress upstream earnings and put pressure on free cash flow, even if some of the shock is absorbed by the more resilient LNG and Power divisions. Volatility in LNG spot prices, particularly in Asia, also remains a challenge. But long-term contracts mitigate this to some extent even if the Group is not immune to periods of sharp price dislocation, especially if global gas balances tighten or regional bottlenecks persist. While the Group's integration and hedging help buffer these effects, a prolonged energy downturn is not impossible, and it could slow deleveraging, delay buybacks, or constrain growth capex.

The second area of risk, is the geopolitical uncertainty and project execution with key growth projects such as Mozambique LNG, which remain dependent on the stabilization of the security situation in Cabo Delgado. It's important to remember that the Group's historical exposure to Russia, notably through its participation in Arctic LNG 2, has already resulted in a $4.1 billion impairment charge and illustrates the vulnerability of long-cycle projects to sudden geopolitical changes. In addition, future upstream projects in regions such as Iraq, Nigeria and Suriname could face operational delays, expropriation risks or cost overruns which could have setbacks in these jurisdictions could have a significant impact on production schedules and cash flow forecasts and even more if several delays occur simultaneously.

The third area of concern relates to the regulatory and ESG framework. As a major European group, TotalEnergies is increasingly exposed to tougher climate policy. The EU's "Fit for 55" package, carbon border adjustment mechanisms, and stricter emissions targets could drive up compliance costs in its refining, petrochemicals, and electricity businesses. In particular, EU carbon taxes and sustainable aviation fuel requirements could reduce margins or divert investment away from Europe. At the same time, ESG-focused capital flows continue to penalize large oil companies, regardless of their transition efforts. Several institutional investors continue to apply blanket exclusions to fossil fuel companies, limiting additional demand for their shares and potentially weighing on their valuation multiples, even when their fundamentals improve.

And lastly, TotalEnergies' energy transition strategy, while credible and forward-looking, carries significant execution risk. The development of large-scale offshore wind, solar farms, and storage infrastructure is capital intensive and operationally complex. If the Integrated Power division fails to achieve its ROACE targets or suffers delays due to permitting issues, inflationary pressures, or supply chain constraints, the market could lose confidence in its ability to deliver returns comparable to its legacy businesses. This, in turn, would reinforce the valuation discount and reframe the Power segment as a dilutive diversification rather than a value-enhancing one.

Conclusion

Under current conditions, TotalEnergies offers one of the most asymmetric risk/return profiles among large global energy companies. The Group has built a diversified and capital-efficient energy model that is resilient, forward-looking and increasingly cash-generative. If the company continues to implement its strategy in its two pillars, fossil energy and renewable energy, and if macroeconomic conditions remain stable overall, I believe it would be normal to see TotalEnergies' share price rise significantly over the next 6 to 12 months to $70 minimum even if oil and LNG prices remain at current levels.

data.title: Rare Stock Picks In May 2025 - From 27 Discerning Analysts
data.description:
The rebound we mentioned in last month’s article continued in May, with the S&P 500 near the YTD highs. Are there any compelling investment ideas out there that may have flown under your radar?

Below is a list of May Buy recommendations made by analysts who had no other bullish recommendations over the past 3 months.

For your directory assistance, we've classified the opportunities by sector.

Healthcare

Alpha Cognition (ACOG) - Longtime analyst Anders Research says it is a relatively unknown pharmaceutical company that just launched its first commercial product, and appears poised to capitalize on a large and underserved ~7 million-patient Alzheimer’s market. - Alpha Cognition: Launching A Differentiated Drug Into A Large Alzheimer's Market Opportunity

Eledon Pharmaceuticals (ELDN) - Longtime analyst Daron Evans says its lead candidate shows promise in improving kidney function post-transplant, potentially becoming the new standard-of-care, and the upcoming Phase 2 study data in Q4 2025 is a critical milestone, with potential for transformative value. - Eledon: A Company With Good Early Data In Kidney Transplant Rejection And A Full Balance Sheet

uniQure (QURE) - Longtime analyst C. C. Abbott highlights a potential catalyst as its lead program for Huntington's Disease (which currently has no cure or disease-modifying treatment) is planning to file an application via the accelerated approval pathway, while the HD treatment space is estimated to reach $1.4B by 2034. - uniQure: A Buy For Their Lead In Huntington's Program, In Light Of PTC's Data (Editor’s note: Shares have rallied since the article was published)

Senseonics (SENS) - Longtime analyst Federico Cuneo says the risk/reward ratio seems skewed favorably, and the stock has the potential to be a multibagger investment, as it recently introduced a 365-day Continuous Glucose Monitoring system, aiming to disrupt a market dominated by short-term CGMs from DexCom, Abbott Laboratories, and Medtronic. - Revisiting Senseonics After 4 Years: Still A Big Investment Opportunity

Financials

Enova International (ENVA) - Value investor Eli Gooch says it is an overlooked and compelling opportunity given the rapid earnings growth while management's significant ownership and strong execution, along with growing software investments, suggest a developing competitive moat. - Enova International: Growing Too Fast To Ignore

eToro (ETOR) - Longtime analyst Nicholas Kitonyi says strong crypto-driven revenue growth and favorable U.S. regulatory shifts could propel its market cap toward $10B, while the platform's unique social trading features and multi-asset offerings provide a competitive edge over peers. - Why It's Not Too Late To Buy eToro

East West Bancorp (EWBC) - Value investor Bet Size Matters says that despite its strong track record, it trades at just 10x forward earnings while it maintains superior asset quality, conservative loan loss provisioning, and strong profitability metrics. - East West Bancorp: An Undervalued Bank With Durable Strength

Communications

iHeartMedia (IHRT) - Longtime analyst Matthew Smith says shares trade at a level that could lead to an asymmetric payoff for those investors willing to hold for an 18-36 month period, and management's growth initiatives and signs of national advertisers returning to the market suggest potential for solid full-year results. - iHeartMedia: Is A Turnaround Occurring?

CuriosityStream (CURI) - Longtime analyst Edward Schneider, CFA says it has rapidly transformed into a profitable, high-growth company with a scalable business, and the Street is not adapting to the pace of AI Content Licensing revenue growth, creating a buying opportunity. - CuriosityStream: Climbing The Wall Of Worry (Editor’s note: Shares have rallied since the article was published)

Cable One (CABO) - Longtime analyst Nils de Graaf says the significant drop over the past few years is a buying opportunity, its financing is secure, and the low valuation makes it an attractive takeover candidate. - Cable One: Cashcow And Takeover Candidate

IDT Corporation (IDT) - Individual investor Jonquil Capital says even after the rally (driven by substantial de-risking and consistent earnings growth), it is still cheap compared to its sum-of-the-parts value, with catalysts like SG&A reduction, a potential NRS spin-off, and AI-driven innovation. - IDT Corporation: Still Undervalued And Outperforming

Tripadvisor (TRIP) - Longtime analyst Daniel Shvartsman says its long-term story looks appealing and that even a conservative 10x EBITDA multiple results in >100% upside. - Questions Going Into Tripadvisor's Q1 Earnings

The Trade Desk (TTD) - Growth investor LDV Research says it is a market leader, and it continues to take market share, while the strong growth prospects and a higher multiple provides upside. - The Trade Desk: Business Momentum Ahead For This Category Leader

Alphabet (GOOG) - Longtime analyst Rob Barnett says it remains fundamentally undervalued - although digital advertising growth is moderating, GOOG maintains strong margins and market leadership while the Cloud division is gaining ground with improving profitability. - Alphabet: There Is Upside To Be Captured

Technology

RADCOM (RDCM) - Longtime analyst Cobiaman says despite strong fundamentals, it trades at a deep discount to peers, and the analyst expects a rerating as it adds new customers and sustains organic growth. - Radcom: Category Leader Growing At 18% At An 8.8x Cash P/E

Clearwater Analytics (CWAN) - Longtime analyst Donovan Jones is bullish given its robust growth prospects and strategic acquisitions, while it is well-positioned in the growing investment management software market. - Clearwater Analytics: Boosting Vertical Integration Through Recent Acquisitions (Upgrade)

Seagate Technology (STX) - Longtime analyst Kerrisdale Capital Management says it trades at just 11x 2026 earnings, far too low given its growth profile and secular tailwinds while it is well-managed, operating in an oligopolistic competitive landscape, with a track record of smart and shareholder-friendly capital allocation. - Seagate: It's HAMR Time

Consumer Discretionary

Watches of Switzerland (OTCPK:WOSGF) - Longtime analyst Investor Aide says it offers strong value with resilient financials, robust EBIT growth, and a compelling 17% ROCE, and its prime retail locations, and exclusive brand relationships support a bullish long-term outlook. - Watches of Switzerland: Dollar-Cost Averaging May Still Be The Order Of The Day For Luxury Retail Bulls

Whirlpool (WHR) - Individual investor Bela Lakos says traditional price multiples and a dividend discount model suggest WHR is undervalued and despite macroeconomic challenges, it expects top-line growth and improved free cash flow in 2025. - Whirlpool: Why We Believe The Stock Is A Buy After The Q1 Results

Toll Brothers (TOL) - Individual investor Mehmood P. Bakhsh says the luxury housing market recovery and effective cost and debt management strategies position Toll Brothers for significant growth and shareholder value creation in the medium to long term. - Toll Brothers Capital Efficient Growth Model Creates An Attractive Low-Risk, High Return Opportunity For Shareholders

Industrials

Global Industrial (GIC) - Individual investor Sanjeev Vaid says it offers a substantial margin of safety and that despite near-term economic headwinds and integration risks, Private Label growth and cost control can unlock significant upside. - Global Industrial: Still Undervalued Despite Recent Missteps

Sunrun (RUN) - Longtime analyst Seth Glucksman says the estimated book value, driven by contract assets, is significantly higher than market cap, while rising utility electricity rates give RUN room to expand margins and cover cost increases. - Don't Run From Sunrun: Why I'm Bullish (Editor’s note: Shares have fallen since the article was published)

Energy

Uranium Energy (UEC) -Longtime analyst ZMK Capital says it is uniquely positioned with U.S.-based assets, a large uranium stockpile, and zero debt, offering high leverage to uranium prices while global uranium demand is rising due to nuclear expansion and supply remains constrained. - Uranium Energy Corp: A Strategic Bet On Nuclear's Resurgence?

Vista Energy (VIST) - Value investor Alan Galecki says it offers superior growth, lower costs, and higher margins compared to Big Oil, making it a compelling alternative for energy sector investors. - Vista Energy: My Preferred Oil Stock Due To Key Competitive Advantages Against Big Oil

Utilities

Central Puerto (CEPU) - Longtime analyst Zach Schweinsberg says its expansion into renewables and mining, plus vast land holdings, positions it as a diversified, forward-thinking energy company in Argentina. - Central Puerto Positioned For Growth Amid Volatility

Engie (OTCPK:ENGIY) - Individual investor Ellsworth Research says the stock price weakness presents a buying opportunity given the long-term growth anticipated from new capacity and strategic investments. - Engie: Best Energy Transition Utility

Real Estate

Host Hotels & Resorts (HST) - Longtime analyst Philip Wang says it boasts one of the strongest balance sheets among lodging REITs, and it offers attractive income from the well-covered 5%+ dividend yield. - Host Hotels & Resorts: Fortress Balance Sheet, 5% Yield Make This A Buy

Inclusion Criteria: The above ideas were objectively compiled based on the set criteria. Exclusions include new Seeking Alpha analysts, broad market calls, crypto ideas, leveraged ETFs, and stocks that were repeat recommendations by the analyst.

Thanks for reading.
